title: On certain combinatorial expressions of TASEP transition probabilities
abstract: We study combinatorial structures arising from finite-time transition probabilities of the Totally Asymmetric Simple Exclusion Process with open boundary conditions. While much of the existing combinatorial theory regarding the TASEP concerns the steady-state distribution, we focus instead on the transient dynamics. We first show that the enumeration of transition sequences between two configurations of the open TASEP is equivalent to the enumeration of standard Young tableaux of a family of non-classical shapes which have been of recent interest in the combinatorial literature. This extends to the open-boundary setting the correspondence between the TASEP with periodic boundaries and cylindric tableaux.   We then introduce a family of tableau-like objects associated with Young diagrams in which repetitions of cells are allowed, subject to the partial order induced by the diagram. For each diagram, we collect the numbers of these objects into an exponential generating function. We prove that the entries of the homogeneous open TASEP transition matrix can be expressed as signed sums of such generating functions over suitable families of diagrams. This gives a combinatorial and order-theoretic interpretation of finite-time transition probabilities for the open TASEP, analogous to the combinatorial mappings known for steady-state probabilities.
summary: This paper provides a deep combinatorial framework for calculating the time evolution of the TASEP on a finite lattice. It achieves this by showing that transition probabilities can be expressed as signed sums over generating functions derived from generalized Young tableaux. This offers a powerful, non-equilibrium mathematical interpretation analogous to known steady-state results.
main_problem: To derive a combinatorial and order-theoretic interpretation for the finite-time transition probabilities of the Totally Asymmetric Simple Exclusion Process (TASEP) with open boundary conditions.
main_result: The transition probability matrix elements are expressed as signed sums involving exponential generating functions associated with generalized Young tableaux counts.
method: The authors relate the time evolution operator $e^{Ht}$ to counting paths (words in local generators) and map these counts bijectively to enumerations involving multisets of cells in Young diagrams.
model_or_system: The system is the TASEP, a model of hard-core particles hopping on a 1D lattice with open boundaries. The dynamics are governed by a Continuous-Time Markov Chain (CTMC) whose transition matrix is $P(t) = e^{Ht}$.
key_observables: Finite-time transition probabilities $\langle P(t) angle_{ij}$ and the associated exponential generating functions $F_D(t)$.
important_parameters: The time parameter $t$, the lattice size $N$, and the boundary conditions (open).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on decomposing the local generators and assuming absolute convergence to rearrange the infinite sums into the final combinatorial form.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate diagrams in $\mathcal{D}_{N+1}^{\eta, \xi}$ and show connections between TASEP paths and standard Young tableaux counts.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the combinatorial equivalence for transition sequences to generalizing this to the full transition matrix, culminating in a final summation formula for $\langle P(t) angle_{ij}$ over specific diagram families.
why_interesting: The connection between non-equilibrium statistical mechanics models (like TASEP) and deep combinatorial structures (like Young tableaux) provides powerful mathematical tools for analyzing open quantum systems dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Panel a) shows in white and light blue the infinite diagram Z13, while in blue only a sub diagram having for outline the path ξ. Panel b) shows two examples (one in light blue only and one in both light and dark blue) of diagrams in D13 η,ξ.

Figure 2: Panel a) shows a standard Young tableau associated with a diagram of the form D2 \ D1, where D2 (represented as blue and white cells) and D1 (represented as only blue cells) are both in D3. Panel b) represents the path in the 2-sites TASEP configuration space in bijection with the standard Young tableaux of panel a).
Figure 3: Panel a) shows an example of a word s in Σ9, where Σ = {hi}5 i=1, b) one of its possible reduced words and c) the labeled poset associated with the equivalence class of s.
